data.content_ref: inline:{"claim": "Selective ideological rigidity: Hittite and Mycenaean kingship tied martial legitimacy to chariot warfare and ritualized single combat, making adaptation to infantry-based 'Sea Peoples' tactics socially unthinkable; Egyptian theology permitted Pharaoh to delegate battlefield command while retaining cosmic kingship", "mechanism": "Institutional path dependence in military-ritual complex: chariot aristocracies cannot renegotiate status hierarchy without delegitimizing core extractive relationships. Egyptian 'division of the sacred' between Re-Horakhty (cosmic order) and Montu/Seth (martial chaos) allows tactical outsourcing.", "scope": {"covers": ["Hittite Anatolia", "Mycenaean Greece", "Egypt"], "excludes": ["Ugarit", "Levantine city-states"]}, "forbidden": [{"case": "Hittite texts describing successful adoption of Sea Peoples infantry tactics by Hittite forces", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Egyptian evidence of royal execution of generals for military defeat during 1208-1177 BC (Merneptah-Ramesses III)", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}, {"case": "Mycenaean pictorial kraters or sealings depicting non-chariot elite warfare after LH IIIB", "eval": "rubric:std-hist"}], "prose_notes": "Builds on but contra 351a1f9c777acd7b14905cc879ce09c13b95ce9545956dae5d0dfa3ec5941032: not merely theological delegation but structural incompatibility of chariot-aristocracy with infantry democratization. Ugarit excluded as commercial not martial palace system."}
